description: Enumerations for the three Genettian dimensions of narrative temporality: order, duration, and frequency.

narrative_order.description: Temporal order of events — the relationship between chronological story sequence (histoire) and narrative presentation sequence (récit).

values.definition: Events narrated in the order they occur in the story world (histoire = récit).
values.example_usage: Default linear narrative; events A, B, C narrated as A, B, C.

values.definition: Retrospective narration: a scene covers events that occurred before the current story-time point (flashback).
values.example_usage: A character remembers a childhood event; a chapter covers backstory.

values.definition: Anticipatory narration: a scene covers events that will occur after the current story-time point (flashforward or foreshadowing).
values.example_usage: A prologue scene from the story's end; narrative anticipation of future events.

values.definition: Multiple simultaneous timelines are interwoven across the narrative, with the reader moving between them.
values.example_usage: Dual or triple timelines alternating chapters; time-jump structure.

values.definition: Narration begins in the middle of the action, with earlier events established through exposition or analepsis.
values.example_usage: Opening action scene followed by backstory; Homer's Iliad structure.

duration_mode.description: Story time vs. discourse time relationship — how much story time is covered by how much text.

values.definition: Story time approximately equals discourse time; real-time dramatic scene. (ST ≈ DT)
values.example_usage: A fully dramatized conversation; a real-time action sequence.

values.definition: Story time is compressed into brief discourse time; many events covered quickly. (ST > DT)
values.example_usage: 'Three months passed.' A chapter covers a year in a few paragraphs.

values.definition: Story time passes but discourse time is zero; a gap in the narration. (DT = 0)
values.example_usage: A chapter break where years elapse; '***' indicating time skipped.

values.definition: Discourse time advances but story time is stopped; description or reflection. (ST = 0)
values.example_usage: A descriptive passage; a meditation; a narrator's long digression.

values.definition: Discourse time exceeds story time; slow-motion expansion of a brief event. (DT > ST)
values.example_usage: A slow-motion fight scene; stream-of-consciousness rendering of a split second.

frequency.description: How many times an event occurs in the story world versus how many times it is narrated.

values.definition: An event occurs once in the story world and is narrated once (1:1). The default narrative mode.
values.example_usage: Most individual scenes; a single dramatized event.

values.definition: Events occur many times in the story world but are narrated as a single generic instance (n:1). ('Every morning she would...')
values.example_usage: Characterizing habitual behavior; establishing background social conditions; conveying passage of time through routine.

values.definition: An event occurs once in the story world but is narrated multiple times, potentially from different perspectives (1:n).
values.example_usage: Rashomon structure; the same event revisited by different characters or narrators.

values.definition: Multiple instances of similar events each narrated separately (n:n).
values.example_usage: A character's many similar arguments narrated individually; serial repetition with variation.
